title: Control and coordination in mammals
summary: This lesson explores the fundamental mechanisms of control and coordination in mammals, focusing on the nervous system. It details the structure of neurones, the generation of nerve impulses through resting and action potentials, and the process of synaptic transmission.
learningObjectives: Describe the structure of a myelinated motor neurone and relate its structure to its function.
Explain the establishment and maintenance of the resting potential in a neurone.
Describe the sequence of events in the generation and propagation of an action potential.
Outline the mechanism of synaptic transmission at a cholinergic synapse and explain its significance.

simpleExplanation.title: The Nerve Impulse: An Electrical Relay
simpleExplanation.summary: Neurones transmit information using rapid electrical signals called action potentials. This process relies on controlled movements of ions across the neurone's membrane, creating a wave of electrical change.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a line of dominoes set up. Tipping the first one (a stimulus) creates a wave of falling dominoes that travels down the line (the action potential). Each domino only moves a short distance, but the wave travels far. After the wave passes, you must reset the dominoes (repolarisation) before another wave can be sent.
steps: Resting State (-70mV): The neurone is 'off' but ready. The sodium-potassium pump actively moves 3 Na⁺ ions out for every 2 K⁺ ions in, creating a negative charge inside the axon.

Firing Signal (Depolarisation): A stimulus opens voltage-gated sodium ion channels. Na⁺ ions rush in, causing the membrane potential to rapidly become positive (around +40mV). This is the action potential.
Resetting (Repolarisation): The sodium channels close and voltage-gated potassium ion channels open. K⁺ ions move out of the axon, causing the potential to become negative again.
Passing the Message (Synapse): At the axon terminal, the electrical signal triggers the release of chemical messengers (neurotransmitters) which diffuse to the next cell, starting a new signal.

flashcards.back: The potential difference across the membrane of a neurone when it is not being stimulated (approx. -70mV), with the inside being negative relative to the outside.
A rapid, temporary reversal of the membrane potential from negative to positive, that is transmitted along the axon of a neurone. It is the basis of a nerve impulse.
The change in potential difference across a neurone's membrane from negative to positive, caused by the influx of sodium ions (Na⁺) into the axon.
The restoration of the resting potential after depolarisation, caused by the efflux of potassium ions (K⁺) out of the axon.
A brief period where the membrane potential becomes more negative than the resting potential, caused by K⁺ channels remaining open slightly too long. This contributes to the refractory period.
A fatty insulating layer surrounding the axon of many neurones, formed by Schwann cells. It speeds up nerve impulse transmission.
The 'jumping' of an action potential from one node of Ranvier to the next in a myelinated neurone, which is much faster than continuous propagation in an unmyelinated neurone.
A synapse that uses acetylcholine (ACh) as its neurotransmitter. Commonly found at the neuromuscular junction.

An action potential is only generated if the stimulus reaches a certain threshold potential. If the threshold is reached, a full-sized action potential is produced; if not, nothing happens. The strength of the stimulus does not affect the size of the action potential.
A short period after an action potential during which the axon cannot be stimulated to generate another action potential. It ensures impulses are unidirectional and limits their frequency.
An enzyme located in the synaptic cleft that breaks down acetylcholine into ethanoic acid (acetate) and choline. This stops the stimulation of the postsynaptic membrane.
The minimum membrane potential that must be reached (approx. -55mV) for an action potential to be generated. It is the potential at which voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are triggered to open.

faq.q: Why is the action potential described as 'all-or-nothing'?
faq.a: The 'all-or-nothing' principle means that if a stimulus is strong enough to depolarise the membrane to the threshold potential (around -55mV), a full-sized action potential will be generated. If the stimulus is weaker and fails to reach the threshold, no action potential occurs at all. The size of the action potential is constant and does not depend on the strength of the stimulus. The brain interprets the strength of a stimulus by the frequency of action potentials, not their amplitude.

faq.q: What is the difference between a neurone and a nerve?
faq.a: A neurone (or nerve cell) is a single, specialised cell that transmits nerve impulses. A nerve is a bundle of axons from many different neurones, all wrapped together in a protective sheath of connective tissue. Think of a single neurone's axon as one copper wire, and a nerve as a large cable containing hundreds of those wires.

In myelinated neurones, the myelin sheath acts as an electrical insulator, preventing ion flow across the membrane. Action potentials can only be generated at the gaps, the nodes of Ranvier. The local circuit of current flows from one node to the next, causing the action potential to 'jump' along the axon. This is much faster than the continuous, step-by-step depolarisation of the entire membrane that occurs in unmyelinated neurones.

sections.content: Mammals possess two major systems for control and coordination: the nervous system and the endocrine system. The nervous system provides rapid, short-lived, and highly specific communication via electrical impulses, ideal for immediate responses like reflexes. The endocrine system uses chemical messengers called hormones, which travel in the blood, resulting in slower, longer-lasting, and more widespread responses. This lesson focuses on the fundamental unit of the nervous system: the neurone, and how it transmits information.
The Structure of a Myelinated Motor Neurone

A motor neurone transmits impulses from the central nervous system (CNS) to an effector, such as a muscle or gland. Its structure is highly adapted for this function. The cell body contains the nucleus and most organelles, with extensions called dendrites that receive signals. A long, single axon fibre carries the impulse away from the cell body. This axon is covered by a myelin sheath, an insulating layer made of Schwann cells, which is interrupted at regular intervals by gaps called nodes of Ranvier.

When a neurone is not transmitting an impulse, it is in a state of 'readiness' known as the resting potential. This is an electrical potential difference of approximately -70 millivolts (mV) across the axon membrane, with the inside being negative relative to the outside. This potential is actively established and maintained by the sodium-potassium pump ($Na^+/K^+$ pump), which uses ATP to pump three sodium ions ($Na^+$) out of the axon for every two potassium ions ($K^+$) it pumps in. The membrane is also more permeable to $K^+$ ions than to $Na^+$ ions due to the presence of more open $K^+$ channels, so $K^+$ ions tend to diffuse out down their concentration gradient, further contributing to the negative charge inside.

items: The $Na^+/K^+$ pump actively transports 3 $Na^+$ out and 2 $K^+$ in.
The axon membrane is more permeable to $K^+$ than $Na^+$ at rest.
The inside of the axon is negative relative to the outside (approx. -70mV).
This electrochemical gradient represents stored potential energy.

sections.content: The Action Potential: The Nerve Impulse

An action potential is a rapid and transient depolarisation and repolarisation of the axon membrane. It is triggered when a stimulus causes the membrane potential to reach a threshold value (around -55mV). At the threshold, voltage-gated $Na^+$ channels open, causing a rapid influx of $Na^+$ ions down their electrochemical gradient. This influx reverses the membrane potential to about +40mV (depolarisation). Almost immediately, the $Na^+$ channels inactivate and voltage-gated $K^+$ channels open. $K^+$ ions then diffuse out of the axon, bringing the membrane potential back down (repolarisation). The membrane may briefly become more negative than the resting potential (hyperpolarisation) before the $Na^+/K^+$ pump restores the resting state.

sections.question: An action potential travels along a myelinated axon of length 1.2 m. The time taken for the impulse to travel from the cell body to the axon terminal is 10 ms. Calculate the speed of conduction in m s⁻¹.
sections.solution:
Step 1: Identify the given values.
Distance (d) = 1.2 m
Time (t) = 10 ms

Step 2: Convert units to be consistent (ms to s).
Time (t) = 10 ms = 10 × 10⁻³ s = 0.010 s

Step 3: Use the formula for speed.
Speed = Distance / Time

Step 4: Substitute the values and calculate.
Speed = 1.2 m / 0.010 s
Speed = 120 m s⁻¹

Final Answer: The speed of conduction is 120 m s⁻¹.

sections.content: Be precise with your terminology. Use 'sodium ions' and 'potassium ions', not just 'sodium' or 'potassium'. Always specify 'voltage-gated' channels when discussing action potentials. Quoting correct values for potentials (e.g., -70mV, +40mV) will gain you marks in structured questions.

Synapses are junctions between neurones, or between a neurone and an effector. At a cholinergic synapse, the arrival of an action potential at the presynaptic terminal triggers the opening of voltage-gated calcium ion ($Ca^{2+}$) channels. The influx of $Ca^{2+}$ causes vesicles containing the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) to fuse with the presynaptic membrane, releasing ACh into the synaptic cleft by exocytosis. ACh molecules diffuse across the cleft and bind to specific receptor proteins on the postsynaptic membrane. This binding opens ligand-gated sodium ion channels, causing an influx of $Na^+$ and leading to depolarisation of the postsynaptic membrane, generating an excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP). If the EPSP reaches the threshold, a new action potential is generated in the postsynaptic neurone.

items: Arrival of action potential opens $Ca^{2+}$ channels.
Influx of $Ca^{2+}$ causes vesicles to release acetylcholine (ACh) by exocytosis.
ACh diffuses across the synaptic cleft and binds to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane.
Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) in the cleft breaks down ACh to stop the signal.

sections.question: Organophosphate insecticides work by irreversibly inhibiting the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE). Explain the effect this would have at a neuromuscular junction.
sections.solution:
Step 1: State the normal function of AChE.
Acetylcholinesterase breaks down acetylcholine (ACh) in the synaptic cleft, terminating the signal.

Step 2: Describe the effect of the inhibitor.
Inhibition of AChE means that ACh is not broken down and remains in the synaptic cleft.

Step 3: Explain the consequence for the postsynaptic membrane.
ACh will continuously bind to the receptors on the postsynaptic membrane (sarcolemma of the muscle fibre).

Step 4: Describe the overall physiological effect.
This causes continuous opening of sodium ion channels, leading to constant depolarisation of the muscle fibre membrane. The muscle will be in a state of continuous, uncontrolled contraction, leading to spasms, fatigue, and eventually paralysis.

Marking points: [1] ACh not broken down/remains in synapse. [2] Continuously binds to receptors. [3] Continuous depolarisation/action potentials in postsynaptic membrane/muscle fibre. [4] Continuous muscle contraction/spasms/paralysis.

sections.content: Questions about synapses often involve applying your knowledge to unfamiliar situations, such as the effects of drugs or toxins. A solid understanding of the normal sequence of events is crucial. Break the problem down: what specific part of the process is being affected, and what are the knock-on effects?

sections.label: Test Your Knowledge on Control & Coordination
